[observation] [info] Isolated files — no import edges between 6 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
[observation] [info] SchedulerEngine is co-located with the app entry point rather than with testable GoToBedCore logic, which may limit unit test coverage of core scheduling behavior.
[pattern] [warning] AppEnvironment is an undocumented composition root — it is the single point where all inter-service wiring happens, but no comment or type annotation marks it as such, increasing the risk that a future contributor adds service coupling elsewhere.
[pattern] [warning] No service protocols exist for SchedulerEngine or OverlayController — AppEnvironment couples concrete types directly, making the composition root difficult to test in isolation.
[suggestion] [info] Zone "App Lifecycle" (app-lifecycle) has at-risk risk (score: 0.50, cohesion: 0.00, coupling: 0.00) — approaching architectural risk thresholds

</findings>

<insights>

- Isolated files — no import edges between 6 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
- Isolated files — no import edges between 6 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
- SchedulerEngine.swift is classified as a service but lives in the app target alongside the entry point — if it holds significant scheduling logic, migrating it into GoToBedCore would allow it to be unit-tested without the full app target.
- ColorBridging.swift (utility) bridges color types across frameworks but has no obvious lifecycle role; verify it belongs here rather than in the UI zone where it is likely consumed.
- LaunchAtLogin.swift wraps a platform capability requiring entitlement configuration; ensure it has integration test coverage that can detect entitlement regressions across macOS versions.
- SchedulerEngine is co-located with the app entry point rather than with testable GoToBedCore logic, which may limit unit test coverage of core scheduling behavior.
- AppEnvironment acts as the sole composition root: it instantiates Store, SchedulerEngine, and OverlayController in its initializer and wires them together via a single closure (scheduler.onFire → overlay.present), making it the only place where inter-service coupling is expressed.
- No protocol or interface layer exists between the three services owned by AppEnvironment — all references are concrete types, which means tests of the wiring logic require real objects or subclass overrides rather than lightweight mocks.
- AppEnvironment.shared is a singleton injected as an @EnvironmentObject, creating dual ownership semantics — the singleton lifetime and the SwiftUI environment lifetime are different and could diverge if the view tree is torn down without calling AppEnvironment.shutdown().
- The scheduler.onFire closure set in AppEnvironment creates an implicit ordering constraint: OverlayController must be fully initialized before SchedulerEngine fires; there is no guard against a race at launch if the first fire happens before setup completes.
- AppEnvironment is an undocumented composition root — it is the single point where all inter-service wiring happens, but no comment or type annotation marks it as such, increasing the risk that a future contributor adds service coupling elsewhere.
- No service protocols exist for SchedulerEngine or OverlayController — AppEnvironment couples concrete types directly, making the composition root difficult to test in isolation.

</insights>
